content.text: Backstage Software Templates accept structured input from developers through a JSON Schema form, use that input to drive Nunjucks template rendering, and execute a sequence of scaffolder actions — called hooks in common usage — before and after file generation. Designing the right variables and hooks for a GenAI starter template is the difference between a template that produces a fully configured service and one that requires ten manual follow-up steps.

This sub-subtopic covers how to define variables that drive conditional file inclusion, how to validate input through pre-scaffold hooks, how to use post-scaffold hooks to provision LLM credentials and register the catalog entry, and how to write custom scaffolder actions for GenAI-specific setup needs.

attrs.alt: Template variable and hook flow in Backstage scaffolder

content.text: Template variables fall into two categories: identity variables that every service needs, and GenAI-specific variables that drive model routing, GPU provisioning, and eval configuration.
Repo name, catalog entry, resource prefix
Gateway credential scope, catalog tags, Helm values
Conditionally include vector store config and retrieval code
Conditionally add streaming endpoint and SSE config
Defining Variables in the Template YAML
as JSON Schema properties. Backstage renders the schema as a multi-step wizard form. Complex fields use custom UI fields (

# catalog/templates/genai-service.yaml  — parameters section
spec:
  parameters:
    - title: Service Identity
      required: [name, owner, costProject, lifecycle]
      properties:
        name:
          title: Service name (slug)
          type: string
          pattern: '^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,40}$'
          description: Used as repo name, Docker image tag, and Kubernetes namespace
          ui:autofocus: true
        owner:
          title: Owning team
          type: string
          ui:field: OwnerPicker
          ui:options:
            catalogFilter:
              kind: Group
        costProject:
          title: GCP cost project code
          type: string
          description: "Used as billing label on all cloud resources"
        lifecycle:
          title: Service lifecycle
          type: string
          default: experimental
          enum: [experimental, production]
          enumNames: ['Experimental (exploratory, no SLA)', 'Production (SLA-backed)']
        slackChannel:
          title: Slack alert channel
          type: string
          pattern: '^#[a-z0-9-]+$'
          default: '#platform-alerts'

    - title: GenAI Configuration
      required: [llmModels]
      properties:
        llmModels:
          title: LLM models to access
          type: array
          minItems: 1
          items:
            type: string
            enum: [gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet, gemini-1.5-pro, llama-3-70b]
          uniqueItems: true
          ui:widget: checkboxes
        gpuClass:
          title: GPU class (for inference or fine-tuning)
          type: string
          default: none
          enum: [none, a10g, h100]
          enumNames: ['None (LLM via gateway only)', 'A10G (inference server)', 'H100 (large model fine-tuning)']
        ragEnabled:
          title: Enable RAG (vector store + retrieval)
          type: boolean
          default: false
          ui:widget: radio
        streamingEnabled:
          title: Enable streaming responses (SSE)
          type: boolean
          default: false
        evalThreshold:
          title: Eval pass rate threshold
          type: number
          default: 0.95
          minimum: 0.5
          maximum: 1.0
          ui:widget: range

Not every file should be emitted for every scaffolding run. A service with RAG needs a vector store client and retrieval module; one without does not. The Backstage scaffolder uses Nunjucks templating in both file content and directory structure to conditionally include files.
-style values. Files inside a directory prefixed with
in the skeleton are included only when the condition is true.

# Skeleton structure in the template repository
skeleton/
├── src/
│   ├── app/
│   │   └── main.py.njk
│   ├── models/
│   │   ├── gateway_client.py.njk
│   │   └── ${{ 'retrieval.py.njk' if values.ragEnabled }}
│   └── prompts/
│       ├── system.txt.njk
│       └── ${{ 'rag_context.jinja2.njk' if values.ragEnabled }}
├── config/
│   ├── settings.yaml.njk
│   └── ${{ 'vector-store.yaml.njk' if values.ragEnabled }}
└── infra/
    ├── terraform/main.tf.njk
    └── ${{ 'gpu-pool.tf.njk' if values.gpuClass !== 'none' }}
file, Nunjucks syntax substitutes variables and emits conditional blocks. The scaffolder processes all
files through the Nunjucks engine before writing output.
Pre-scaffold hooks run before any files are written. For GenAI templates, three validations are critical: GPU quota check, team name validation, and service name availability. These use the built-in

steps:
    - id: check-gpu-quota
      name: Check GPU quota
      action: http:backstage:request
      input:
        method: POST
        path: /api/platform/quota/check
        body:
          team: ${{ parameters.owner }}
          gpuClass: ${{ parameters.gpuClass }}
          requestedCount: 1
      # If the platform API returns 4xx, the step fails and scaffolding stops

    - id: check-name-available
      name: Check service name availability
      action: http:backstage:request
      input:
        method: GET
        path: /api/catalog/entities/by-name/component/default/${{ parameters.name }}
        # Returns 404 if available; 200 means the name is taken
      # Note: Backstage scaffolder treats non-2xx as failure — catch 404 as success
      # via a custom action (see below)

    - id: validate-cost-project
      name: Validate cost project code
      action: http:backstage:request
      input:
        method: GET
        path: /api/platform/billing/projects/${{ parameters.costProject }}

The name-availability check requires a custom scaffolder action because a 404 is the success case (name is free). Writing a custom action is the correct pattern whenever the built-in HTTP action's error semantics do not match the validation logic.
Custom scaffolder actions are Node.js modules installed into the Backstage backend. They receive the template's
(the directory where files will be written) and can call external APIs, read/write files, or throw to fail the scaffold.
Post-Scaffold Hooks: Credential Provisioning and Notification

After the repository is created, a post-scaffold hook calls the LLM gateway admin API to create a scoped credential for the new service. This ensures every scaffolded service has LLM access configured before the developer writes their first line of code.

The final post-scaffold hook sends a Slack message to the team channel with links to the new repository, the Backstage catalog entry, and the provisioned credential name — so the team has everything they need in one notification.
block exposes values from step outputs as links in the Backstage scaffolding success screen. Well-designed outputs let developers navigate directly to their new service without hunting for URLs.

output:
    links:
      - title: GitHub Repository
        url: ${{ steps['create-repo'].output.remoteUrl }}
        icon: github
      - title: Backstage Catalog Entry
        url: https://backstage.internal/catalog/default/component/${{ parameters.name }}
        icon: catalog
      - title: Provisioning PR (infra)
        url: ${{ steps['open-infra-pr'].output.remoteUrl }}
        icon: scaffolder
    text:
      - title: LLM Credential Secret
        content: ${{ steps['provision-llm-cred'].output.credentialSecretName }}
Too many variables on one page
Form becomes overwhelming; teams skip optional fields
Use multi-step wizard with 3–5 fields per page
Name collisions or quota overruns discovered only after repo creation
Run name-check and quota-check as the first two steps
All scaffolded services break on gateway migration
Read gateway URL from Backstage app-config, not the template YAML
Credential provisioning fails; developer has no recourse
Make post-hooks idempotent; document manual recovery steps in the template README
Teams leave critical fields empty; CI breaks later
Provide sensible defaults for all optional fields; mark only truly optional ones
Effective GenAI template customization combines three elements:
Well-typed variables with clear descriptions and sensible defaults — identity variables for all services plus GenAI-specific variables (llmModels, gpuClass, ragEnabled, evalThreshold)
Nunjucks conditional rendering — include RAG modules, streaming endpoints, and GPU Terraform only when the corresponding variable is enabled
Pre- and post-scaffold hooks — validate input before writing files (quota check, name availability) and provision resources after (LLM credentials, catalog registration, Slack notification)

Custom scaffolder actions are the escape hatch when built-in actions cannot express the required validation or provisioning logic — write them as typed TypeScript modules, register them in the scaffolder plugin, and keep them idempotent so retries are safe.
